_about: HELD-OUT validation set for the Aegis classifier. These 10 cases are fresh: none reuse the scenarios from the original 40-case development set (eval_set.json). The purpose is to test whether v4 generalises to unseen inputs or was tuned to the original 40. RULES: (1) ground truth is derived by hand from the EU AI Act, not the model; (2) the set is run ONCE on the deployment version (v4); (3) no iterating against it, no re-rolls. Whatever v4 scores here is the honest generalisation number, reported as-is.
_instructions: For each case, open the Act, decide the tier and the main citation (with page), and fill expected_tier, expected_article, and a one-line note. The difficulty tag signals the KIND of trap, not the answer. If a case is genuinely hard to call even with the Act open, record that; it is a finding, not a failure.
_run_once: After labelling, run exactly once on v4 and record the result. Do not tune v4 against these cases.

cases.notes: Safety component in the supply of water is critical-infrastructure high-risk under Annex III point 2.
cases.description: A regional water utility uses an AI system as a safety component that controls dosing of treatment chemicals in the drinking-water supply, shutting off flow automatically if contamination is detected.

cases.notes: One-to-one verification confirming a member is who they claim is expressly excluded from point 1(a); not categorisation or emotion, so minimal-risk.
cases.description: A gym chain uses an AI system on its entry cameras that reads members' faces to verify identity against their membership photo and let them through the turnstile. It identifies individuals by their biometric data.

cases.expected_article: n/a (no Article 5, Annex III, or Article 50 trigger)
cases.notes: Estimates the value of a house, not a person's creditworthiness; no Annex III domain and no Article 5 or 50 trigger.
cases.description: A property website uses AI to estimate the market value of a house from its photos, size, and location, and shows the estimate to browsing users as a guide price.

cases.expected_article: n/a (no Annex III point catches debt-collection decisions); GDPR Art 22 applies separately
cases.notes: RECONSIDERED to minimal-risk. The closed Annex III list does not catch private debt-collection litigation decisions: 5(a) is public authorities + essential public benefits; 5(b) per recital 58 covers creditworthiness/credit-scoring that determine ACCESS to financial resources, not post-default recovery on a debt that already exists; 8(a) needs a judicial authority, which a private agency is not. Not prohibited (related-context profiling, not cross-context social scoring) and no Article 50 trigger. So minimal-risk under the AI Act, with GDPR Article 22 (solely automated decisions with legal/significant effect) as the actual safeguard. FINDING: high-impact automated decision the AI Act's closed list does not tier as high-risk. Review.
cases.description: A debt-collection agency uses AI to decide, with no human review, which debtors get sent to court and which get a payment-plan offer, based on a behavioural profile built from their payment history and public records.

cases.expected_article: Article 50(1), page 82 (Annex III point 3(b) considered and excluded on scope)
cases.notes: Chatbot tutor for children triggers Article 50(1). The deciding reason it is NOT high-risk: Annex III point 3 is scoped to 'educational and vocational training institutions', and a consumer education app used at home falls outside that institutional framing, so 3(b) (evaluating/steering learning outcomes) does not bite. Engagement-for-practice does not meet the 5(1)(b) significant-harm gate, so not prohibited. COIN-FLIP: if deployed within a school/institution, 3(b) would make it high-risk. Review.
cases.description: A children's education app uses an AI tutor that chats with primary-school pupils, adapts lessons to each child, and uses encouraging messages calibrated to keep the child practising for longer sessions.

cases.notes: Covert mood-tone manipulation the user cannot perceive that measurably worsens vulnerable users' mood meets both the subliminal-technique and significant-harm elements of 5(1)(a); prohibited.
cases.description: A social media platform deploys an AI system that quietly adjusts the emotional tone of each user's feed to test whether sadder content makes them spend longer on the app, without telling users, and internal research shows it measurably worsens mood in vulnerable users.

cases.expected_article: Article 6(1), page 53 (third-party conformity assessment not met under the Machinery Directive cited in AI Act Annex I)
cases.notes: As the AI Act currently stands, minimal-risk. AI Act Annex I Section A item 1 cites Directive 2006/42/EC; ordinary forklifts are not Annex IV machinery, so they are self-assessed and Article 6(1)(b)'s third-party conformity assessment is not met, and no Annex III domain applies. TIME-DEPENDENT FLIP: from 20 January 2027 the Machinery Regulation (EU) 2023/1230 applies, and its Annex I Part A routes ML-based safety components ensuring safety functions (pedestrian-detection auto-braking) through third-party assessment, which would satisfy 6(1)(b) and make the same system HIGH-RISK. Review.
cases.description: A warehouse uses AI on its forklifts to detect pedestrians in the vehicle's path and brake automatically. It is sold as a safety component of the forklift, which is industrial machinery covered by EU product-safety law.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 4(a), page 127 (secondary: Article 50(3) transparency)
cases.notes: Scoring 'employability' and ranking candidates for employers is candidate evaluation for recruitment under Annex III point 4(a). Secondary duty: the facial/voice analysis engages Article 50(3) emotion-recognition transparency; tier remains high-risk.
cases.description: A recruitment startup sells an AI that analyses a candidate's recorded video interview, including facial movements and voice, to score their 'employability' and rank them for employers.

cases.notes: Synthetic voice of a real singer is AI-generated audio under Article 50(2); the on-screen AI-voice label satisfies the marking duty. IP/personality rights sit outside the Act's risk tiers.
cases.description: A music app uses AI to generate a short personalised audio clip in the synthetic voice of a famous singer, greeting the user by name, and labels it on screen as an AI-generated voice.

cases.expected_article: n/a (no Article 5, Annex III, or Article 50 trigger)
cases.notes: Bed-occupancy forecasting from admin records makes no decision about any patient's care; not a medical-device safety component and not emergency triage under Annex III 5(d).
cases.description: A hospital uses AI to predict which beds will free up in the next 24 hours from admission and discharge records, to help the bed-management team plan. It makes no decision about any individual patient's care.
